heading: Scoring Behavior
body:
By default, the text operator uses the `bm25` similarity
algorithm to score documents depending on their relevance to the query.

You can change which similarity algorithm is used by specifying a
different similarity algorithm in the `similarity.type` property for
the string fields in your MongoDB Search index definition. To learn how to
configure a MongoDB Search index for the string type, see
How to Index String Fields.

To learn more about the supported similarity algorithms, see
[Score Details](fts-similarity-algorithms).

The `autocomplete` operator query results that are exact matches
receive a lower score than results that aren't exact matches. MongoDB Search
can't determine if a query string is an exact match for an indexed text
if you specify just the autocomplete-indexed token substrings. To score
exact matches higher, try the following workaround:

> **Note:** The following workaround doesn't guarantee higher scores for exact
> matches in all cases.

- Index the field as both [tokenization](bson-data-types-autocomplete) and [string](bson-data-types-string) types.

  MongoDB Search `autocomplete` boosts exact matches when an `autocomplete`
  field is also indexed as a `string`, thereby increasing the score
  of exact matches.

- Query using the [compound](compound-ref).

For a demonstration of this workaround, see
[Search Across Multiple Fields](autocomplete-compound).
